title: Boundary Anomaly Inflow Requires Chiral Edge Modes
summary: On a manifold with boundary, the gauge variation of the bulk integer Chern-Simons response cannot be canceled by a purely local boundary counterterm, so anomalous gapless boundary modes are required to absorb the inflow.
assumptions: The bulk effective action contains an integer-level Chern-Simons term.
The system is defined on a manifold with physical boundary.
regime: Integer quantum Hall boundary theory and its continuum anomaly-cancellation story.
scope: Source-grade anomaly-inflow theorem reused in the Lecture Three edge-mode family.

source_anchors.notes: Witten's anomaly-inflow statement for the integer Hall boundary.
Original source for anomaly inflow and wall-localized chiral zero modes.

representation: Reusable anomaly-inflow theorem linking the bulk Chern-Simons response to mandatory chiral boundary degrees of freedom.
motivation: This note exists because Lecture Three needs a theorem-level statement of anomaly inflow, not only the local cancellation equation. The theorem says that a nontrivial bulk Hall response on a manifold with boundary forces chiral edge degrees of freedom.
prerequisite_story: Know the integer Hall-response theorem, the parity-anomaly half-level ingredient, and the Callan-Harvey equality between the bulk boundary variation and the edge anomaly.
local_context_story: In the current topic graph, this theorem sits between the explicit Callan-Harvey fragment and the final domain-wall edge-mode theorem. It abstracts the physical message of the cancellation identity.
forward_links_story: Combined with the Jackiw-Rebbi wall-mode existence theorem, this anomaly-inflow theorem yields the final statement that a sign-changing Dirac mass supports a chiral edge mode consistent with the bulk Hall level.
canonical_questions: Why does a bulk Chern-Simons term on a manifold with boundary force edge modes?
How does the inflow equation become a theorem about net boundary chirality?
Why is this theorem distinct from the separate lattice Nielsen-Ninomiya no-go statement?
scope_boundaries: This theorem is a continuum bulk-boundary consistency statement and should not be confused with the lattice charge-cancellation theorem.
It assumes the bulk integer Hall response is already known and does not independently derive that bulk integer.

math_expressions.notes: Bulk Chern-Simons action for the integer Hall phase.
Gauge variation becomes a boundary anomaly that must be canceled by edge degrees of freedom.
Callan-Harvey inflow equation equating the edge anomaly with the bulk variation.
Net boundary chirality must match the bulk Hall level.

symbols.meaning: integer Hall-response level multiplying the Chern-Simons action
gauge parameter whose variation exposes the boundary anomaly
boundary chiral current whose divergence carries the edge anomaly
net chirality of boundary gapless modes

failure_modes: This continuum anomaly-inflow statement does not by itself prove the separate lattice Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem.
admissible_assumptions: Work with an integer-level Hall response on a manifold with boundary in the continuum effective theory.
Treat the local boundary anomaly formula as available input through the Callan-Harvey lemma.
formalization_blockers: The formal relation between the bulk Hall level and net boundary chirality still needs a reusable index-style wrapper in Lean.
The current KB still keeps the continuum theorem separate from the lattice regularization story instead of connecting them with a common formal interface.
retrieval_hints: Use when the answer must explain why a nontrivial bulk Hall term forces chiral boundary modes.
